planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; mealy blue sage is a native Texas perennial often grown as an annual in zones north of 7, widely sold as the 'Victoria Blue' bedding plant — one of the most heat-tolerant, long-blooming sages for Gulf Coast and Southern landscapes and an outstanding butterfly and hummingbird plant.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Salvia farinacea (mealy blue sage, mealycup sage) is a native Texas perennial sage found naturally on limestone outcrops, cedar glades, and dry prairies from central Texas into New Mexico; it takes its common name from the floury white (farinaceous) coating on the calyces that cradle the spikes of brilliant true-blue to violet-blue flowers, one of the few true, pure-blue flowering plants in the southern landscape. Remarkably heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant, blooming continuously from spring through hard frost in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens without deadheading; 'Victoria Blue' is the most widely sold selection, an AAS winner. Perennial in zones 7–11; self-seeds prolifically; outstanding for butterfly and hummingbird gardens. Can be used as a fresh-cut or dried flower.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring germination in the South and Gulf Coast, where it blooms in spring and early summer; Monarda citriodora is a Texas and Great Plains native annual with strongly lemon-scented foliage and whorled spikes of pink to lavender flowers that are among the finest nectar sources for native bees.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface in fall in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 12 inches in spring. Monarda citriodora (lemon beebalm, lemon mint) is a widespread native annual or short-lived perennial of the central grasslands from Texas to Kansas and east to the Gulf Coast, growing on dry prairies, roadsides, and disturbed sites in calcareous, well-drained soils; it is the most aromatic of the native beebalms, with strongly lemon-scented foliage that can be used as a culinary and tea herb. The whorled flower spikes produce rings of pink to lavender flowers with distinctive purple-spotted bracts at intervals along the stem in late spring and early summer — among the most important early nectar sources for native bees including bumblebees and Monarda specialist bees. Naturalizes readily from self-seeding in dry, sunny sites; a superb plant for wildflower meadows and native bee gardens throughout the South.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Coral Nymph is a distinctive bicolor selection of the native tropical sage with salmon-pink and white two-toned flowers, more delicate in appearance than standard red S. coccinea and outstanding in heat-tolerant garden designs for the Gulf Coast and Deep South.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after last frost into well-drained, moderately fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade. Coral Nymph is a bicolor-flowered cultivar of the native American Salvia coccinea (tropical sage), producing distinctive two-toned flowers with salmon-coral upper lips and soft white lower lips on the same spikes — a gentler, more pastel coloration than the vivid red of the species that combines well with soft-colored perennial plantings. Like all S. coccinea cultivars, it is heat- and humidity-tolerant in the extreme, blooming continuously through Gulf Coast and Southern summers without deadheading, and self-seeds prolifically so that a single planting effectively perpetuates itself. Outstanding for hummingbird and butterfly gardens; annual in most zones but naturalized and perennial in zone 9–11.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; common rush is among the most important native emergent wetland plants for rain gardens, bioswales, pond margins, and stormwater management areas, providing erosion control, wildlife shelter, and sediment filtration.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist to saturated, slightly acidic to neutral soil; common rush tolerates partial flooding and is excellent for rain garden center zones and pond margins. Juncus effusus (common rush or soft rush) is a widespread native wetland plant found in marshes, wet meadows, stream margins, and pond edges throughout North America; the smooth, cylindrical, deep-green stems (the photosynthetic organs — leaves are reduced to sheaths at the base) emerge in dense, arching clumps 2–4 feet tall and produce branched, brownish flower clusters appearing to emerge from the side of the stem. Common rush is one of the most ecologically valuable native wetland plants for rain garden and constructed wetland design — providing erosion control, bird nesting material, and insect habitat. The cultivar 'Spiralis' (corkscrew rush) with spiral, twisted stems is widely grown as an ornamental water garden plant and is one of the most distinctive ornamental grasses for containers and bog gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in shallow water (0–12 inches deep) or consistently saturated soil in full sun; narrow-leaved cattail is more graceful and less aggressively spreading than the common broadleaf cattail, making it a better choice for ornamental water gardens and small pond margins where containment matters.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or bare-root plants in spring in 0–12 inches of standing water or in consistently saturated soil at pond margins; in containers, use aquatic planting baskets to control spread. Typha angustifolia (narrow-leaved or lesser cattail) is similar to but more graceful than the common broadleaf cattail (T. latifolia), with narrower strap-like leaves, a slightly shorter stature (4–6 feet), and a distinctive gap between the upper (male) and lower (female) portions of the brown, sausage-shaped flower spike — in T. latifolia the two parts are contiguous. The narrow-leaved cattail is somewhat less aggressively rhizomatous than T. latifolia, making it a better choice for controlled pond margin and water garden plantings. Cattails provide critical wildlife habitat (red-winged blackbird nesting, marsh wren nesting, feeding waterfowl), superior water quality improvement through sediment trapping and nutrient uptake, and the familiar brown seed heads iconic in naturalistic landscape design.
